title: Classes & Objects
summary: Learn how to build your own custom data types using classes — bundling data and behavior together in one tidy package.

blocks.md:
So far you have used Python's built-in types: strings, lists, numbers. But what if you want to create your **own** type — something that models a real-world thing, like a dog, a bank account, or a player in a game?

That is exactly what **classes** are for. A class lets you define a brand-new kind of object with its own data and its own actions.

blocks.title: Cookie Cutter vs. Cookies
blocks.md:
A **class** is like a cookie cutter — it is just the shape. An **object** (also called an *instance*) is an actual cookie made from that cutter.

You define the class once. Then you can stamp out as many individual objects as you like, each with its own data.
Use the `class` keyword followed by the class name (by convention, use **CapitalizedWords**). The body of the class is indented, just like a function.

blocks.code:
class Dog:
    pass  # an empty class for now

my_dog = Dog()  # create an instance
print(type(my_dog))
blocks.caption: Even an empty class can be instantiated.

blocks.md: `Dog()` with parentheses **creates** (instantiates) a new object from the class. The variable `my_dog` now holds that object. `type()` confirms it belongs to your `Dog` class.

blocks.text: Attributes — Storing Data on an Object

blocks.md:
An **attribute** is a piece of data attached to a specific object. You define attributes inside a special method called `__init__` (pronounced "dunder init"), which Python calls automatically whenever you create a new instance.

Think of `__init__` as the "set up" instructions that run the moment a cookie is stamped out.

blocks.code:
class Dog:
    def __init__(self, name, breed):
        self.name = name
        self.breed = breed

dog1 = Dog("Bella", "Labrador")
dog2 = Dog("Max", "Poodle")

print(dog1.name)
print(dog2.breed)
blocks.caption: Each instance keeps its own copy of the attributes.

blocks.title: What is `self`?
blocks.md:
`self` is a reference to **the specific object being worked on right now**.

When you write `dog1 = Dog("Bella", "Labrador")`, Python secretly passes `dog1` as the first argument. `self.name = name` means "store `name` on *this particular* dog".

You must include `self` as the first parameter of every method — Python handles passing it automatically.
A **method** is a function that lives inside a class. It describes what an object can *do*. Methods always take `self` as their first parameter so they can access the object's own data.

blocks.output:
Bella says: Woof!
Bella is a Labrador.
blocks.caption: Call a method using dot notation: object.method()

blocks.md: Notice that when you call `dog1.bark()` you do **not** pass `self` — Python does that for you. You only write `self` in the method *definition*, not when you call it.
Every object created from a class has its own separate copy of the attributes. Changing one object does not affect another.

blocks.code:
dog1 = Dog("Bella", "Labrador")
dog2 = Dog("Max", "Poodle")

dog1.name = "Bella-Boo"  # change only dog1

dog1.describe()
dog2.describe()
blocks.output:
Bella-Boo is a Labrador.
Max is a Poodle.
blocks.caption: Modifying dog1 leaves dog2 completely untouched.

blocks.md: Here is a slightly bigger class to show how attributes and methods work together naturally.

blocks.output:
Deposited $50. New balance: $150
Withdrew $30. New balance: $120
Not enough funds!
blocks.caption: The class bundles the balance (data) with deposit/withdraw (behavior).

blocks.title: Forgetting `self` in a Method
blocks.md:
A very common mistake is forgetting `self` as the first parameter of a method:

```python
class Dog:
    def bark():          # WRONG — missing self
        print("Woof!")
```

When you call `dog1.bark()`, Python will raise a `TypeError` complaining about too many arguments. Always start every method with `self`.

blocks.title: Why Bother with Classes?
blocks.md:
Without classes, a program managing 50 dogs would need 50 separate variables for names, 50 for breeds, and so on — hard to keep track of.

With a class, each dog carries **all its own data** in one place, and you get the same helper methods for free on every instance. Code stays organized and reusable.

blocks.question: What is the purpose of the `__init__` method in a class?
options: It runs when you delete an object.
It sets up an object's attributes when the object is first created.
It is required for the class to have any methods at all.
It prints a summary of the object.
blocks.explanation: `__init__` is called automatically the moment you create a new instance (e.g. `Dog("Bella", "Labrador")`). Its job is to initialize the object's attributes so the object starts in a well-defined state.

keyTakeaways: A **class** is a blueprint; an **object** (instance) is a real thing built from that blueprint.
`__init__` runs automatically when you create an instance — use it to set up attributes with `self.attribute = value`.
`self` always refers to the specific object a method is currently working on.
Methods are just functions inside a class — they give objects behavior and always take `self` as their first parameter.
OOP keeps related data and behavior bundled together, making code easier to read, reuse, and maintain.

exercise.prompt: Create a class called `Cat` with the attributes `name` and `color`. Give it two methods: `meow()` which prints "<name> says: Meow!", and `describe()` which prints "<name> is a <color> cat." Then create two different Cat instances and call both methods on each.
exercise.hint: Use `self.name` and `self.color` inside `__init__` to save the values. In your methods, access those saved values with `self.name` and `self.color`.
exercise.solution:
class Cat:
    def __init__(self, name, color):
        self.name = name
        self.color = color

    def meow(self):
        print(f"{self.name} says: Meow!")

    def describe(self):
        print(f"{self.name} is a {self.color} cat.")

cat1 = Cat("Luna", "black")
cat2 = Cat("Mochi", "orange")

cat1.meow()
cat1.describe()
cat2.meow()
cat2.describe()
